--- Cold re-query of the viewport±margin after a wholesale buffer content
--- swap — undo, redo. Unlike an edit, there's NO TSInputEdit to give
--- the lane (the old_tree it retained is for pre-undo text and can't be
--- incrementally shifted to post-undo text), so we dispatch with
--- has_edit=false (cold parse of just the viewport margin buckets).
--- The lane replaces doc_state.old_tree with the fresh tree, so the
--- NEXT edit resumes incremental parsing as usual.
---
--- Clears the whole cache + in-flight + pending (all spans are stale
--- vs the swapped text), bumps _hl_gen so any in-flight async response
--- is treated as stale (gen mismatch → dropped), refreshes total bytes,
--- dispatches the cold viewport query, then sync-waits so post-undo render
--- shows correct spans (zero-flash) on files where the lane responds
--- within the cap; on large files it falls back to the translated-less
--- (here: empty) cache for a frame and the async install lands shortly.
---@param fallback_byte integer? cursor byte to anchor the viewport query on
function View:_hl_cold_requery(fallback_byte)
    if not self._hl_enabled then
        return
    end
    self._hl_bucket_cache = {}
    self._hl_names = {}
    self._hl_in_flight = nil
    self._hl_pending = nil
    self._hl_starts_cache = nil
    self._hl_starts_gen = nil
    self._hl_gen = self._hl_gen + 1
    self:_hl_refresh_total_bytes()
    local lo_b, hi_b = self:_hl_viewport_margin_bucket_range(fallback_byte)
    self:_hl_dispatch(lo_b, hi_b, false, nil, true)
    self:_hl_wait_response()
end

--- Compute the total document byte length and update bucket math.
--- Called from render before bucket dispatch.
function View:_hl_refresh_total_bytes()
    local b = self.buffer
    local total = 0
    local count = b:line_count()
    for i = 0, count - 1 do
        total = total + b:line_len(i)
    end
    self._hl_total_bytes = total
end

--- Number of buckets covering the current document.
---@return integer
function View:_hl_bucket_count()
    return math.floor((self._hl_total_bytes + BUCKET_BYTES - 1) / BUCKET_BYTES)
end

--- The editor calls this at the top of each render with the visible
--- window's absolute byte range. `_hl_tick` (called from
--- highlight_segments) uses it to decide which viewport buckets to query.
---@param start_byte integer
---@param end_byte integer
function View:_hl_notify_viewport(start_byte, end_byte)
    self._hl_last_vstart = start_byte
    self._hl_last_vend = end_byte
end

--- The bucket range covering viewport ± HL_MARGIN_BUCKETS. Edits only
--- re-query this bounded range (the visible truth + a safety margin so
--- error-recovery effects just outside the window are caught); the
--- translated cache serves as a good-enough color bridge for everything
--- off-screen until it scrolls into view. Falls back to the edit's own
--- bucket when no viewport has been notified yet (e.g. cold start).
---@param fallback_byte integer|nil start byte to anchor on if no viewport
---@return integer lo_b, integer hi_b
function View:_hl_viewport_margin_bucket_range(fallback_byte)
    local vstart = self._hl_last_vstart
    local vend = self._hl_last_vend
    local lo_b, hi_b
    if vstart == nil or vend == nil then
        local b = bucket_of(fallback_byte or 0)
        lo_b = b
        hi_b = b + 1
    else
        lo_b = bucket_of(vstart)
        hi_b = bucket_of(math.max(vend - 1, vstart)) + 1
    end
    lo_b = lo_b - HL_MARGIN_BUCKETS
    hi_b = hi_b + HL_MARGIN_BUCKETS
    if lo_b < 0 then
        lo_b = 0
    end
    local total = self:_hl_bucket_count()
    if hi_b > total then
        hi_b = total
    end
    if hi_b < lo_b then
        hi_b = lo_b
    end
    return lo_b, hi_b
end

function View:undo()
    if not self.buffer:undo() then
        return false
    end
    self:clamp_cursor()
    -- Undo swaps buffer content wholesale; the cached spans (and the
    -- lane's retained old_tree) describe pre-undo text. Cold-requery
    -- the viewport so post-undo render shows correct syntax.
    local c = self:p()
    local starts = self:_hl_line_starts()
    local byte = (starts[c.line + 1] or 0) + c.col
    self:_hl_cold_requery(byte)
    return true
end

function View:redo()
    if not self.buffer:redo() then
        return false
    end
    self:clamp_cursor()
    local c = self:p()
    local starts = self:_hl_line_starts()
    local byte = (starts[c.line + 1] or 0) + c.col
    self:_hl_cold_requery(byte)
    return true
end

--- Clamp every cursor to valid range after undo/redo.
function View:clamp_cursor()
    local lc = self:line_count()
    for _, c in ipairs(self.cursors) do
        if c.line >= lc then
            c.line = math.max(0, lc - 1)
        end
        local cl = self:content_len(c.line)
        if c.col > cl then
            c.col = cl
        end
        c.goal_col = c.col
        c.visual_col = nil
        c.yank_line = nil
        c.yank_col = nil
    end
end

function View:delete_selection()

    -- n_by_cursor[c] = chars in this cursor's region (0 if no selection).
    local n_by_cursor = {}

--- cursors); for deletes it's the OR of each cursor's will_join test

--- fresh one; otherwise ensure a group is open (begin if none). The
--- group is left OPEN afterwards — persistent across consecutive

--- or a breaking keystroke closes/restarts it, via this same function
--- or via each_cursor's close_edit_for_motion.

--- This is the only order that composes correctly with NEWLINE-CREATING
--- edits (an insert at line L that splits the line shifts cursors below
--- L down; if we'd already processed a later cursor, its coordinates
--- would be stale). Processing forward means each edit only needs to
--- translate cursors that come AFTER it in document order.

function Buffer.new()
    local ptr = ffi.cast("struct Buffer *", c.calloc(1, ffi.sizeof("struct Buffer")))
    if ptr == nil then
        error("cursed: failed to allocate Buffer", 2)
    end
    ptr.lines = ffi.cast("struct Line *", c.calloc(64, ffi.sizeof("struct Line")))
    if ptr.lines == nil then
        c.free(ptr)
        error("cursed: failed to allocate Buffer lines", 2)
    end
    ptr.cap = 64
    ptr.count = 0

    local self = setmetatable({
        _ptr = ptr,
        _munmapped = false,
        _gc_guard = nil,
    }, Buffer)

    -- Seed exactly one empty line: every line in this model carries a
    -- trailing '\n' (content length = line_len - 1). A buffer with zero
    -- lines violates the editor invariant and crashes on first edit
    -- (line_len: index 0 out of range [0,0)). This covers both the
    -- no-file path and the loaded-empty-file path, since main.lua keeps
    -- this Buffer (rather than calling from_mmap) for 0-byte files.
    local nl_off = self:append_add("\n")
    self:grow_lines(1)
    self:init_line(0, 1, nl_off, 1)
    ptr.count = 1

    -- GC finalizer: cleanup then free (guard must stay alive as long as self)
    self._gc_guard = gc.wrap_gc(ffi.new("uint8_t[1]"), function()
        buffer_cleanup(self)
    end)

    return self
end

--- Create a Buffer from an mmap'd orig region (IO lane handoff).
--- Takes ownership of the mmap'd data — caller must NOT munmap it.
---@param data any mmap'd pointer
---@param len integer File size in bytes
---@param cap integer Capacity (page-rounded) in bytes
---@return Buffer
function Buffer.from_mmap(data, len, cap)
    local self = Buffer.new()
    local b = self._ptr

    -- Transfer ownership of the mmap'd region (cast void* → uint8_t*)
    b.orig.data = ffi.cast("uint8_t *", data)
    b.orig.len = len
    b.orig.cap = cap

    -- Set up GC guard for auto-munmap
    self._munmapped = false
    -- Build per-line piece tables from the mmap'd content
    self:build_lines_from_orig()

    return self
end

    return self._ptr.orig.data

    return self._ptr.orig.len

--- Zero-flash on-screen path: synchronously wait (busy-poll the lane's
--- inbox_hl) for the just-dispatched query to land and be installed,
--- up to HL_SYNC_WAIT_MS. By installing the real spans BEFORE the
--- post-edit render, the user never sees the semantically-stale
--- translated cache for the edited region (translate only byte-shifts
--- existing spans — it can't know the new syntax).
---
--- The lane already owns the parser/query/old_tree (single source of
--- truth — no main-side parser duplication); we just wait for it, off
--- the keyloop, while it parses the viewport±margin incrementally.
--- Busy-polls the ring via editor:drain_hl_inbox rather than blocking
--- on the kqueue so we DON'T consume EVFILT_USER wakes that the main
--- loop's select() still needs to observe for inbox_io/resize — any
--- leftover inbox_hl wake is harmlessly re-drained by the main loop
--- after we've already popped the message.
---
--- Circuit breaker: HL_SYNC_STALL_LIMIT consecutive timeouts disable
--- the sync path so a slow/dead lane can't add latency on every
--- keystroke. Re-enabled when an async install lands (lane caught up).
---
--- Only called when _hl_record_edit actually dispatched (no in-flight
--- was pending). If a query was already in flight at edit time, the
--- edit is queued as pending and we can't wait for it (it hasn't been
--- sent yet) — that path falls back to the async translate bridge.

--- Delete n signed characters from every cursor as one undo group.
--- Grouping is decided once by OR-ing each cursor's will_join test
--- (a delete that joins lines is structural and breaks the group).
---
--- Returns (rl, rc, { el, ec }) from fn: the buffer's post-edit result
--- for THIS cursor plus the PRE-edit end of the deleted region.
--- batch_edit then builds the unified delete translator that pulls
--- post-region cursors up and collapses in-region cursors to the
--- result. Handles single-line, cross-newline-join, and the
--- cursor-inside-a-deleted-span case uniformly.
---@param n integer signed character count
function View:delete_char(n)
    if n == 0 then
        return
    end
    local buf = self.buffer
    -- Aggregate will_join across cursors: any structural delete breaks
    -- the group. Preserves single-cursor semantics exactly (N=1 →
    -- "any joins" == "the one joins").
    local any_join = false
    for _, c in ipairs(self.cursors) do
        local content_len = buf:line_len(c.line) - 1
        local will_join
        if n > 0 then
            will_join = c.col + n > content_len
        else
            will_join = c.col + n < 0
        end
        if will_join then
            any_join = true
            break
        end
    end
    self:batch_edit(any_join, function(c)
        -- Compute the pre-edit region end BEFORE the buffer mutates,
        -- since _delete_char_impl walks a changing line_count.
        local el, ec = delete_region_end(buf, c.line, c.col, n)
        local rl, rc = buf:delete_char(c.line, c.col, n)
        -- Normalize so the returned region is [start, end) half-open with
        -- start = min(cursor pre-edit point, region end) and end = the
        -- other. For forward deletes cursor pos is the start; for backward
        -- deletes the region end (below the cursor) is the start.
        local sl, sc
        if n > 0 then
            sl, sc = c.line, c.col
        else
            sl, sc = el, ec
            el, ec = c.line, c.col
        end
        return sl, sc, rl, rc, { el, ec }
    end)
    self:_set_goal_col(self:p().col)
end

#!/usr/bin/env python3
import os, pty, time, sys, select

BIN = "./build/cursed"
F = "/tmp/cursed_repro.txt"
with open(F, "w") as fh:
    fh.write("hello world\nfoo bar baz\n")

# keystrokes as termbox key strings. We send raw terminal escape sequences.
# cursed uses termbox2 which reads from the tty. We must send bytes that
# termbox decodes into the right keys.
KEYS = {
    "C-space": b"\x00",
    "right":   b"\x1b[C",
    "left":    b"\x1b[D",
    "C-_":     b"\x1f",       # ctrl-_ produces 0x1f
    "C-x":     b"\x18",
    "u":       b"u",
    "d":       b"d",
    "X":       b"X",
    "C-c":     b"\x03",
    "C-a":     b"\x01",
    "C-e":     b"\x05",
}

def seq(*names, delay=0.15):
    out = b""
    for n in names:
        out += KEYS[n]
    return out, delay

def main():
    pid, fd = pty.fork()
    if pid == 0:
        os.execv(BIN, [BIN, F])
    buf = bytearray()
    log = open("/tmp/cursed_pty.log", "wb")
    def drain(t):
        end = time.time() + t
        while time.time() < end:
            r,_,_ = select.select([fd], [], [], 0.05)
            if r:
                try:
                    d = os.read(fd, 65536)
                except OSError:
                    break
                if not d: break
                buf.extend(d); log.write(d); log.flush()
    # scenario: set mark, move right 5, type X (replaces selection), then undo
    actions = [
        ([b"\x00"], 0.3),                 # set mark
        ([b"\x1b[C"]*5, 0.3),             # right x5
        ([b"X"], 0.5),                    # type X (replaces selection)
        ([b"\x1f"], 1.0),                 # ctrl-_ undo
    ]
    time.sleep(0.6); drain(0.5)
    for frames, delay in actions:
        for fr in frames:
            os.write(fd, fr)
            time.sleep(0.05)
        drain(delay)
    # get final screen: send a redraw? Just read what we have.
    time.sleep(0.3); drain(0.5)
    # search the raw buffer for "command error"
    text = buf.decode("utf-8","replace")
    # crude: strip escapes
    import re
    plain = re.sub(r"\x1b\[[0-9;?]*[A-Za-z]", "", text)
    plain = re.sub(r"\x1b[()][AB0]", "", plain)
    if "command error" in plain or "arithmetic" in plain:
        print("ERROR DETECTED")
    # print last 2000 chars
    print(plain[-1500:])
    # quit
    try: os.write(fd, b"\x18\x18\x18")
    except: pass
    time.sleep(0.2)
    try: os.close(fd)
    except: pass
    os.waitpid(pid, os.WNOHANG)

main()

    with open("/tmp/cursed_repro.txt","w") as f:

    pid, fd = pty.fork()
    if pid == 0:
        fcntl.ioctl(0, termios.TIOCSWINSZ, WINSIZE)
        os.execv(BIN, [BIN, "/tmp/cursed_repro.txt"])
    fcntl.ioctl(fd, termios.TIOCSWINSZ, WINSIZE)
    log = bytearray()
    def drain(t):
        end=time.time()+t
        while time.time()<end:
            r,_,_=select.select([fd],[],[],0.03)
            if r:
                try: d=os.read(fd,65536)
                except OSError: break
                if not d: break
                log.extend(d)
    time.sleep(0.6); drain(0.4)
    for b,label in seq:
        os.write(fd,b); time.sleep(0.06); drain(0.35)
    time.sleep(0.2); drain(0.6)
    try: os.write(fd, b"\x18\x18")
    except: pass
    time.sleep(0.15)
    try: os.close(fd)
    except: pass
    try: os.waitpid(pid,0)
    except: pass
    text = log.decode("utf-8","replace")
    plain = re.sub(r"\x1b\[[0-9;?]*[A-Za-z]","",text)
    plain = re.sub(r"\x1b[()][AB0]","",plain)
    return plain

    err = ""

    # extract a window around 'error' if present
    idx = plain.find("command error")
    snippet = plain[idx-5:idx+120] if idx>=0 else ""
    print(f"=== {name}: {'ERROR!! '+err if err else 'ok'}")

name: tmux
description: Use this tmux skill for launching persistent background processes, interacting with or automating TUI applications via key injection, reading TUI interfaces programmatically, and in all other ways interacting with interactive terminal interfaces designed for a human.
---

# Tmux Skill: Persistent Session Management for Agent Automation

## Description

Comprehensive guide to using tmux for launching persistent background processes, automating TUI (Terminal User Interface) applications via key injection, and reading TUI output programmatically. This skill enables agents to interact with long-running command-line applications, automate interactive CLI tools, and monitor terminal-based interfaces without visual dependency.

## Core Concepts

tmux is a terminal multiplexer that enables:
- **Persistent Sessions**: Programs continue running even when SSH disconnects or the terminal closes
- **Programmatic Control**: Send keystrokes, capture output, and control pane contents via command-line interface
- **Background Execution**: Run processes that survive terminal detachment and agent restarts
- **TUI Automation**: Interact with ncurses, text-mode applications, and interactive CLIs

## Prerequisites

- tmux 3.0+ (tested with 3.6a)
- Basic understanding of tmux sessions, windows, and panes hierarchy

---

## 1. Launching Programs in Sessions (Persistent Background Execution)

### Creating Detached Sessions

The key to persistent background execution is creating tmux sessions that don't attach to the current terminal.

#### Basic Detached Session

```bash
# Create a new session without attaching
tmux new-session -d -s mysession

# Create with a specific command
tmux new-session -d -s mysession 'long-running-script.sh'

# Create with working directory
tmux new-session -d -s mysession -c /path/to/dir 'python myapp.py'
```

**Flags:**
- `-d`: Detach session (don't attach to current terminal)
- `-s name`: Session name for easy reference
- `-c path`: Set working directory
- `-x cols -y lines`: Set terminal dimensions (important for TUIs)

#### Session Persistence Options

```bash
# Create session with specific dimensions (critical for TUI apps)
tmux new-session -d -s mytui -x 80 -y 24 'htop'

# Create session with environment variables
tmux new-session -d -s mysession -e "API_KEY=secret" -e "DEBUG=1" 'node server.js'

# Create session that won't exit when command finishes
tmux new-session -d -s mysession 'tail -f /var/log/syslog'
```

#### Starting the tmux Server Non-Daemon Mode

For debugging or when you want the server in foreground:

```bash
tmux start-server \;  # Starts server
tmux -D               # Don't daemonize (exit with terminal)
```

### Managing Multiple Sessions

```bash
# List all sessions
tmux list-sessions

# Attach to an existing session
tmux attach-session -t mysession

# Kill a session
tmux kill-session -t mysession

# Kill all sessions except specified
tmux kill-session -a -t mysession
```

### Starting Multiple Programs in One Session

```bash
# Create session and split into panes
tmux new-session -d -s dev 'vim main.py' \; \
  split-window -d -h 'python main.py' \; \
  split-window -d -v 'tail -f server.log' \; \
  select-pane -t 0
```

### Running Services as Detached Processes

```bash
# Start a web server in background
tmux new-session -d -s webserver -x 80 -y 24 'python -m http.server 8000'

# Start database monitor
tmux new-session -d -s dbmonitor -x 120 -y 36 'mongosh --eval "db.serverStatus()"'

# Start file watcher
tmux new-session -d -s watcher 'inotifywait -m -r /path/to/watch'
```

---

## 2. Sending Key Presses to Panes (TUI Automation)

### The send-keys Command

`send-keys` sends keystrokes to a specific pane, enabling automation of interactive applications.

#### Basic Keystroke Sending

```bash
# Send literal keys to pane 0 of current session
tmux send-keys -t 0 'ls -la' Enter

# Send Ctrl+C to all panes in a session
tmux send-keys -t mysession: C-c

# Send to specific pane in a session
tmux send-keys -t mysession:0.0 'vi file.txt' Enter
```

**Syntax:** `tmux send-keys [-t target-pane] [keys...]`

**Key Notation:**
- `C-x`: Ctrl+key (e.g., `C-c` for Ctrl+C)
- `M-x`: Alt/Meta+key (e.g., `M-d` for Alt+D)
- `Enter`: Return/Enter key
- `Space`: Space key
- `Tab`: Tab key
- `Up`, `Down`, `Left`, `Right`: Arrow keys
- Literal strings sent as-is

#### Targeting Panes

```bash
# Target by pane index
tmux send-keys -t 0 'command'

# Target by pane ID
tmux send-keys -t %0 'command'

# Target by session:window.pane notation
tmux send-keys -t mysession:1.2 'command'

# Target all panes in a session
tmux send-keys -t mysession: 'ls -la' Enter

# Target marked pane
# First mark a pane: tmux select-pane -m
# Then send to marked: tmux send-keys -t '{marked}'
```

#### Advanced Keystroke Examples

```bash
# Send multi-line command
tmux send-keys -t 0 'cat > /tmp/test.sh << EOF' Enter \
  '#!/bin/bash' Enter \
  'echo "Hello"' Enter \
  'EOF' Enter

# Navigate vim using tmux automation
tmux send-keys -t vim_session:0.0 ':w' Enter ':q!' Enter

# Interact with htop
tmux send-keys -t htop_session:0.0 q  # Quit htop

# Send special characters
tmux send-keys -t 0 C-u  # Kill line (bash/readline)
tmux send-keys -t 0 C-l  # Clear screen
tmux send-keys -t 0 M-b  # Backward word (bash)
```

#### Sequencing Multiple Commands

Use semicolons to chain tmux commands:

```bash
# Create session, run command, send keys
tmux new-session -d -s automate 'htop' \; \
  send-keys -t automate:0.0 q \; \
  send-keys -t automate:0.0 'top' Enter
```

#### Repetition with -N Flag

```bash
# Send 'x' 10 times
tmux send-keys -t 0 -N 10 'x'
```

#### Literal Mode with -l Flag

```bash
# Send keys literally without interpretation
tmux send-keys -l -t 0 'C-x'  # Sends literal C-x, not Ctrl+X
```

### Practical TUI Automation Examples

#### Automating tmux Configuration

```bash
# Create a panel and send setup commands
tmux new-session -d -s setup 'bash' \; \
  send-keys -t setup:0.0 'echo "export PATH=$PATH:/opt/bin" >> ~/.bashrc' Enter \
  send-keys -t setup:0.0 'source ~/.bashrc' Enter
```

#### Automating Terminal-Based Editors

```bash
# Session for vim automation
tmux new-session -d -s editor -x 80 -y24 'vim /tmp/test.txt' \; \
  send-keys -t editor:0.0 'iHello World' Escape ':wq' Enter
```

#### Automating Interactive Installers

```bash
# Automate apt-get install (non-interactive mode preferred, but example for interactive)
tmux new-session -d -s installer -x 80 -y24 'apt-get install vim' \; \
  send-keys -t installer:0.0 'y' Enter  # Confirm installation
```

#### Menu Navigation Example

```bash
# Navigate a TUI menu
tmux new-session -d -s menu_app -x 80 -y24 './menu_app' \; \
  send-keys -t menu_app:0.0 Down Enter \;  # Move down and select
  send-keys -t menu_app:0.0 Down Down Enter  # Skip and select third option
```

---

## 3. Reading Pane Contents (TUI Visibility)

### capture-pane Command

The primary method for reading pane contents is `capture-pane`, which captures text from a pane's screen and history.

#### Basic Capture to STDOUT

```bash
# Capture visible pane contents to stdout
tmux capture-pane -p -t 0

# Capture to a variable
output=$(tmux capture-pane -p -t 0)
echo "$output"
```

**Flags:**
- `-p`: Print to stdout (instead of buffer)
- `-t`: Target pane
- `-S`: Start line (0 = first visible line, - = start of history)
- `-E`: End line (default = end of visible pane, - = end of visible pane)
- `-a`: Alternate screen (useful for TUIs using alternate screen)
- `-e`: Include escape sequences for colors/attributes
- `-C`: Escape non-printable characters as octal
- `-J`: Join wrapped lines
- `-N`: Preserve trailing spaces
- `-P`: Capture beginning of incomplete escape sequence

#### Capturing History

```bash
# Capture entire history (lines - to - means all)
tmux capture-pane -p -S - -E - -t 0

# Capture last 100 lines from history
tmux capture-pane -p -S -100 -t 0

# Capture specific line range (lines 50-100)
tmux capture-pane -p -S 50 -E 100 -t 0
```

#### Capturing Alternate Screen

Many TUIs use the "alternate screen" (like htop, vim, mc):

```bash
# Capture from alternate screen (what the TUI is displaying)
tmux capture-pane -p -a -t mytui
```

#### Capturing with Formatting

```bash
# Capture including color codes
tmux capture-pane -p -e -t 0

# Capture with special characters escaped
tmux capture-pane -p -C -t 0

# Capture preserving whitespace
tmux capture-pane -p -N -J -t 0
```

### pipe-pane Command (Real-time Monitoring)

`pipe-pane` sends pane output to a shell command, enabling real-time monitoring/datalogging.

#### Basic Piping

```bash
# Pipe pane output to a file
tmux pipe-pane -t 0 'cat > /tmp/pane_output.txt'

# Pipe pane output with session/window/pane ID in filename
tmux pipe-pane -t mysession 'cat > ~/output.#{session_name}.#{window_index}.#{pane_index}'
```

**Flags:**
- `-O`: Connect pane's stdout to command (default)
- `-I`: Connect command's stdout to pane
- `-o`: Only open if no existing pipe (toggle)

#### Real-time Monitoring Example

```bash
# Start logging pane output to file
tmux pipe-pane -t monitoring -o 'cat >> /tmp/app.log'

# Monitor and grep in real-time
tmux pipe-pane -t 0 'grep --line-buffered "ERROR" >> /tmp/errors.log'
```

#### Bi-directional Pipe

```bash
# Pipe pane output AND send commands back to pane
# Create a named pipe
mkfifo /tmp/pane_pipe
tmux pipe-pane -O -I -t 0 'cat /tmp/pane_pipe'

# In another terminal, send commands
echo "ls -la" > /tmp/pane_pipe
```

## 4. Control Mode (Advanced Automation)

Control mode allows programmatic interaction with tmux via a text protocol.

### Starting Control Mode

```bash
# Start tmux in control mode
tmux -C

# From within control mode, send commands
list-sessions
new-session -d -s test
send-keys -t test:0.0 'ls -la' Enter
```

### Control Mode Protocol

Commands are sent via stdin, responses come via stdout with `%begin`/`%end` markers:

```
$ echo "list-sessions" | tmux -C
%begin 1234567890 1 0
0: test (1 panes) [80x24]
%end 1234567890 1 0
```

### Notifications Control Mode Receives

Control mode receives notifications about changes:
- `%client-detached client`
- `%client-session-changed client session-id name`
- `%window-changed window-id`
- `%window-layout-changed window-id`
- `%pane-mode-changed pane-id`
- `%output pane-id text` - Output from pane

Real-time monitoring example:

```bash
# Use control mode to monitor pane output
(tmux -C; wait) <<EOF 2>/dev/null | while read -r line; do
  case "$line" in
    %output*)
      # Extract pane ID and text output
      pane_id=$(echo "$line" | cut -d' ' -f2)
      text="${line#%output $pane_id }"
      echo "[$pane_id] $text"
      ;;
  esac
done
```

## 5. Best Practices for Agent Automation

### Session Naming

Use descriptive, predictable session names:

```bash
# Good naming
tmux new-session -d -s web_monitor '...'
tmux new-session -d -s db_backup_worker '...'
tmux new-session -d -s file_scanner '...'

# Include namespaces for complex setups
tmux new-session -d -s project_name_web '...'
tmux new-session -d -s project_name_worker '...'
```

### Managing Session Lifecycle

```bash
# Check if session exists before creating
if ! tmux has-session -t mysession 2>/dev/null; then
  tmux new-session -d -s mysession 'python app.py'
fi

# Wait for process to start
sleep 2

# Send initial commands
tmux send-keys -t mysession:0.0 'config:reload' Enter
```

### Error Handling

```bash
# Capture command result with error handling
if ! tmux new-session -d -s test 'command'; then
  echo "Failed to create session"
  exit 1
fi

# Verify pane is responsive
count=0
while [ $count -lt 10 ]; do
  output=$(tmux capture-pane -p -t test:0.0)
  if [ -n "$output" ]; then
    break
  fi
  sleep 1
  count=$((count+1))
done
```

### Terminal Size for TUIs

Always set appropriate dimensions for TUI applications:

```bash
# Standard sizes
tmux new-session -d -x 80 -y 24 'htop'

# Larger terminals
tmux new-session -d -x 120 -y 36 'mc'

# Use environment variable
tmux new-session -d -x $COLUMNS -y $LINES 'vim'
```

### Cleanup on Exit

```bash
# Cleanup function
cleanup() {
  tmux kill-session -t mysession 2>/dev/null
  rm -f /tmp/pipe_*
}

trap cleanup EXIT

# Create session
tmux new-session -d -s mysession '...'
```

## 6. Example Workflows

### Example 1: Automated Web Server Monitor

```bash
#!/bin/bash

SESSION=web_monitor

# Create session with web server
tmux new-session -d -s $SESSION -x 80 -y24 'python -m http.server 8000'

# Wait for server to start
sleep 2

# Log output
tmux pipe-pane -t $SESSION 'cat >> /tmp/webserver.log'

# Monitor for specific log messages
while true; do
  output=$(tmux capture-pane -p -t $SESSION)
  if echo "$output" | grep -q "GET.*404"; then
    echo "404 detected at $(date)" >> /tmp/404_alerts.log
  fi
  sleep 1
done
```

### Example 2: Automated Database Backup

```bash
#!/bin/bash

SESSION=db_backup
DB_NAME=production

# Create session for backup
tmux new-session -d -s $SESSION -x 80 -y24 \
  "mongodump --db $DB_NAME --out /backup/$(date +%Y%m%d)"

# Wait for backup to complete
while true; do
  output=$(tmux capture-pane -p -t $SESSION)
  if echo "$output" | grep -q "done dumping"; then
    echo "Backup completed successfully"
    break
  elif echo "$output" | grep -q "error"; then
    echo "Backup failed!"
    tmux kill-session -t $SESSION
    exit 1
  fi
  sleep 5
done

# Cleanup
tmux kill-session -t $SESSION
```

### Example 3: Interactive TUI Test Automation

```bash
#!/bin/bash

SESSION=tui_test

# Start the TUI application
tmux new-session -d -s $SESSION -x 80 -y24 './interactive_menu_app'

# Navigate menu and check results
# Step 1: Select "Settings" (option 1)
tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:0.0 Down Enter
sleep 1

# Step 2: Check we're in settings menu
output=$(tmux capture-pane -p -t $SESSION)
if ! echo "$output" | grep -q "Settings Menu"; then
  echo "Failed to navigate to settings"
  tmux kill-session -t $SESSION
  exit 1
fi

# Step 3: Change an option (simulate arrow keys and Enter)
tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:0.0 Down Enter
sleep 0.5
tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:0.0 '80' Enter

# Step 4: Save and exit
tmux send-keys -t $SESSION:0.0 Escape Esc ':q' Enter

# Verify output
output=$(tmux capture-pane -p -t $SESSION)
echo "$output"

# Cleanup
tmux kill-session -t $SESSION
```

### Example 4: Log Analyzer

```bash
#!/bin/bash

SESSION=log_analyzer
LOG_FILE=/var/log/app.log

# Start tail in tmux
tmux new-session -d -s $SESSION "tail -f $LOG_FILE"

# Pipe output for analysis
tmux pipe-pane -t $SESSION -o \
  'while read line; do echo "$line" | grep "ERROR" && echo "ERROR FOUND"; done | tee /tmp/errors.log'

# Run for specified duration
SECONDS=0
while [ $SECONDS -lt 3600 ]; do
  if [ -s /tmp/errors.log ]; then
    echo "Error detected at $(date)"
  fi
  sleep 5
done

# Cleanup
tmux pipe-pane -t $SESSION ''  # Stop piping
tmux kill-session -t $SESSION
```

### Example 5: Multi-Stage Deployment

```bash
#!/bin/bash

SESSION=deployment

# Stage 1: Build
tmux new-session -d -s $SESSION -x 80 -y24 -n build 'make build'
sleep 30  # Wait for build

# Verify build succeeded
output=$(tmux capture-pane -p -t $SESSION:build.0)
if ! echo "$output" | grep -q "Build successful"; then
  echo "Build failed!"
  exit 1
fi

# Stage 2: Tests (new window)
tmux new-window -t $SESSION -n tests 'make test'
sleep 20

# Verify tests passed
output=$(tmux capture-pane -p -t $SESSION:tests.0)
if ! echo "$output" | grep -q "All tests passed"; then
  echo "Tests failed!"
  tmux kill-session -t $SESSION
  exit 1
fi

# Stage 3: Deploy (new window)
tmux new-window -t $SESSION -n deploy './deploy.sh'
sleep 10

# Verify deployment
output=$(tmux capture-pane -p -t $SESSION:deploy.0)
if echo "$output" | grep -q "Deployment successful"; then
  echo "Deployment completed!"
else
  echo "Deployment failed!"
  tmux kill-session -t $SESSION
  exit 1
fi

# Keep session for inspection
echo "Session '$SESSION' available for inspection"
```

## 7. Reference: Key Commands Summary

### Session Management
- `new-session [-d] [-s name] [-c dir] [-x cols] [-y lines] [command]` - Create session
- `attach-session [-t name]` - Attach to session
- `detach-client` - Detach current client
- `kill-session [-t name]` - Kill session
- `list-sessions` - List sessions

### Window Management
- `new-window [-d] [-n name] [-c dir] [command]` - Create window
- `split-window [-dvh] [command]` - Split pane
- `kill-window [-t target]` - Kill window
- `list-windows [-t session]` - List windows

### Pane Management
- `send-keys [-t target] [keys...]` - Send keystrokes
- `capture-pane [-p] [-S start] [-E end] [-a] [-e] [-C] [-t target]` - Capture output
- `pipe-pane [-O] [-I] [-o] [-t target] [command]` - Pipe output
- `list-panes [-F format] [-t target]` - List panes
- `select-pane [-t target]` - Select pane
- `kill-pane [-t target]` - Kill pane
- `select-layout [layout]` - Set layout

### Output Capture
- `capture-pane -p` - Print to stdout
- `capture-pane -a` - Alternate screen
- `capture-pane -S - -E -` - All history

### Control Mode
- `tmux -C` - Start in control mode
- Commands return with `%begin`/`%end` markers
- Notifications: `%client-detached`, `%window-changed`, `%output`, etc.

### Format Variables
- `#{session_name}` - Session name
- `#{window_index}` - Window number
- `#{pane_index}` - Pane number
- `#{pane_pid}` - Pane process ID
- `#{pane_current_command}` - Current command
- `#{pane_current_path}` - Current directory

## 8. Troubleshooting

### Session Not Found

```bash
# Check if tmux server is running
tmux list-sessions 2>&1

# If server not running, start it
tmux start-server
```

### Pane Not Responding

Verify pane exists and has a running process:

```bash
# Check pane status
tmux display-message -p '#{pane_pid}' -t 0

# List all panes
tmux list-panes -a
```

### Capture Returns Empty

- Ensure `-a` flag is used for alternate screen TUIs
- Check session/window/pane target specification
- Verify pane has content before capturing

### Keys Not Being Sent

- Check target specification
- Verify session name exists
- Use literal mode with `-l` for special characters
- Check for stuck processes in pane

### Pipe Pane Not Working

- Close existing pipes before opening new: `tmux pipe-pane -t target ''`
- Check command syntax and permissions
- Verify shell command works independently

## 9. Security Considerations

### Session Visibility

Sessions persist even after logout:

```bash
# List all sessions (including from other users if permissions allow)
tmux list-sessions

# Restrict socket permissions
chmod 700 $TMUX_TMPDIR/tmux-*
```

### Process Isolation

Panes run as the user who created them. Consider:

- Using dedicated user for automation jobs
- Setting appropriate file permissions on log files
- Monitoring pane commands for security implications

### Input Validation

When automating with dynamic input:

```bash
# Sanitize input before sending keys
# Avoid shell injection
safe_input=$(echo "$user_input" | sed 's/[;&|]/\\&/g')
tmux send-keys -t 0 "$safe_input"
```

  # Continue automation

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Fuzz: try many select/edit/ctrl-x-ctrl-u combos, log errors.
set -u
cd "/Users/alexispurslane/Development/scratch/cursed"
BIN=./build/cursed
F=/tmp/cursed_repro.lua
ERR=/tmp/cursed_err.log

reset_file() {
  printf 'local x = 1\nlocal y = "hello world"\nprint(y, x)\n' > "$F"
}

# args: name, then a sequence of tmux key tokens
run() {
  local name="$1"; shift
  rm -f "$ERR"
  reset_file
  tmux kill-session -t cf 2>/dev/null
  tmux new-session -d -s cf -x 120 -y 40 -c "$PWD" "$BIN $F"
  sleep 0.8
  for k in "$@"; do
    tmux send-keys -t cf:0.0 "$k"
    sleep 0.18
  done
  sleep 0.3
  if [ -s "$ERR" ]; then
    echo "### $name: ERROR"
    cat "$ERR"
    echo "---"
  else
    echo "### $name: ok"
  fi
  tmux kill-session -t cf 2>/dev/null
}

run "sel+Del+CxCu" C
